div.head: 188. Telegram From the Secretary of State to the Department of StateSource: Department of State, Central Files, 751G.00/5–1355. Secret; Limit Distribution. Repeated to Vienna for MacArthur and Robertson and to Saigon for Reinhardt from Dulles. Reinhardt was still in Washington and received the action copy.
div.p:
Secto 46. We have had a very thorough exchange of views with President Faure and the interested French Ministers, with the participation of Foreign Secretary Macmillan. As a result there is, we believe, a good understanding on the part of each of the viewpoint of the other and a fresh basis for useful cooperation to save Free Vietnam. As a result of this exchange of views, the US has formulated the following instructions for the guidance of yourself and all other US official elements in Vietnam: 1.
You will continue to give complete, loyal and sincere support to the present government of Diem and you should deal with it as the independent and sovereign government which we believe it is and should be. 2.
We would like to see that government strengthened. We note that some progress has already been made in that direction. We believe that further strengthening is still possible and desirable. The US has no right and no desire to dictate the composition of the government of an independent and sovereign state. If, however, there is an appropriate occasion, you will inform Premier Diem that the magnitude of the problems confronting the Government of Free Vietnam is such as to call for a government which includes both technical competence and a broad representation. We would not expect the inclusion of those who would not loyally support the government’s program. We would greatly hope that a sense of patriotism would make it possible to organize a government of a truly national character.
In developing this question of strengthening the government, you will maintain contact with your French and British colleagues. 3.
It is the US view that the lawful government should exercise unquestioned authority not only over the National Army but also over the National Police. 4.
The Binh Xuyen should be disbanded, and its dependable elements incorporated into the national life of Vietnam. We believe that there should be a gradual and peaceful amalgamation of sects having a genuine cultural existence into the normal life of Vietnam. You may, as there are appropriate occasions, make known our views in that respect and exert any proper influence in the direction indicated. 5.
You should encourage the development of electoral machinery and representative processes such as are suitable to Vietnam in order
to create a stable support for the national government and make possible orderly governmental changes from time to time. 6.
We deplore the existence of tension as between the various elements which need to cooperate if Vietnam is to preserve its freedom. Therefore, you will exert yourself to bring about a cessation of anti-French propaganda on the part of the Government of Vietnam. We have been assured by the French Government that its attitude is such that a basis exists for the cessation in Vietnam of propaganda from one side or another which only irritates the relations which need to be cordial. 7.
While neither the US nor the Government of Vietnam is a party to the Geneva Armistice Agreement, we believe that both should envisage the holding of free elections in 1956, under genuine conditions of freedom, without intimidation before or reprisals after. Secretary Dulles expressed himself in this sense to Prime Minister Diem when he was in Saigon in March, and this continues to be US policy. 8.
For your information, we understand that there will be a progressive reduction of French forces in Vietnam, which, however, we believe will be at a rhythm consistent with the increasing capabilities of the Vietnam National Armed Forces but that the reduction will come to a pause when some minimum level has been reached. We also understand that a part of the French Expeditionary Corps will be regrouped in the region of Cap Saint-Jacques so that it will be less conspicuous than in the populated Saigon area. You will be advised further of this by your French colleague. We trust that the Government of Vietnam will welcome this arrangement and will not disturb it by now demanding any complete removal of French forces, which we believe would be undesirable at the present time.
We also understand that the French will so order their dealings with the Viet Minh in the north as to avoid any appearance of an equivocal posture. 9.
In conclusion, we emphasize that we do not believe that freedom can be saved in Vietnam if violent revolutionary forces are brought into play. Almost surely the Viet Minh would end up by capturing control of the revolution because this is a process at which they are past masters. We believe that Diem should now be in a position, with the clear support of the French and the US, to adopt moderate and constructive policies, to delegate responsibility without fear that this will endanger his political life and that there may be carried forward with vigor, competence and broad understanding the agreed program for developing a national government which will have the general support of the freedom-loving people of Vietnam and the confidence of friendly foreign governments.
10.
These instructions have been shown to the French and you may show to Ely.This Kidder did, according to telegram 5402 from Saigon, May 21. Kidder found Ely “convinced refusal accept some such political proposition as that advanced by Bao Dai recently marked last opportunity save Vietnam from Communism.” Ely believed that the retention of Diem was “a catastrophic decision.” He confided to the American Chargé that he had requested termination of his mission. LaForest had asked Ely to reconsider, but had authorized him to return to France if he did not wish to remain in Vietnam. (Ibid., 751G.00/5–2155) British also informed.
